fingerprint: Eclipse coverage fractions are globe-derived Besselian element computations; dome formula structurally dependent on spherical Earth eclipse geometry
globe_value: Eclipse coverage fractions (0.95, 0.90, 0.55, 0.42, 0.80, 0.40, 0.72, 0.70, 0.92) computed from Besselian elements assuming spherical Earth at 150M km from Sun, Moon at ~384,000 km
dome_value: Same coverage fractions used directly in dome formula: ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF. No independent dome computation of eclipse geometry.
match_precision: Exact — dome uses globe eclipse predictions as input, not as calibration
why_it_matters: On a flat disc with local Sun at 5,733 km and local Moon at 2,534 km, eclipse shadow geometry would be fundamentally different — the penumbral cone shape, the path across the surface, and the coverage fraction at each location all depend on the relative sizes and distances of Sun, Moon, and the curvature of the Earth's surface. The dome model has no independent eclipse mechanism (the evolution page concedes it 'incorrectly predicted 2024 eclipse magnitudes identically across all latitudes' before V12). Eclipse coverage fractions can only be computed from spherical geometry, making the dome's eclipse prediction formula structurally parasitic on globe computations.

formula_breakdown.baseline: -10.9 nT (BOU 2017) or -22.24 nT (W004 recalibration) — both are globe-measured INTERMAGNET observations
formula_breakdown.coverage: Eclipse obscuration fraction per station — computed from Besselian elements (spherical Earth geometry). Cannot be derived from flat-disc physics.
formula_breakdown.FSF: Claimed as 'field strength factor from V12 H(r)/r dome geometry.' Uses dome's H(r) function, but station radial positions r must be computed from WGS84 coordinates via the d_geo transform (a = π × R_Earth = 20,015 km — itself a globe fingerprint per Section 4.5.9/EXP-003).

detailed_analysis.globe_dependencies_count: 3 of 3 factors in the prediction formula depend on globe infrastructure: baseline (globe-measured observation), coverage (globe-computed eclipse geometry), FSF (requires WGS84→disc coordinate transform). The dome's only original contribution is the H(r) weighting, which itself uses a = π × R_Earth.
detailed_analysis.OPEN_001_admission: OPEN-001 ('Dome-native coordinates without WGS84') remains IN PROGRESS. Until solved, every station position used to compute FSF is computed from globe coordinates. The dome cannot evaluate its own formula without the globe's coordinate system.
detailed_analysis.evolution_page_admission: The evolution page states the dome 'incorrectly predicted 2024 eclipse magnitudes identically across all latitudes' — this failure occurred because the original formula lacked the FSF term. The fix (adding H(r)/r) is itself dependent on r values computed from WGS84.
detailed_analysis.win025_vs_standard_model: WIN-025 cites the Chernogor & Holub (2024) paper, which attributes the ±10 nT variations to standard ionospheric Sq current physics (Chapman 1933). The paper itself uses INTERMAGNET data from globe-positioned stations and explains the results within standard spherical ionospheric physics. The dome model adds no independent prediction or mechanism — it observes a 90-year-old standard physics phenomenon and relabels the cause.

code_search.found_in: monitor.py: Domain 10 'Eclipse Magnetic Anomaly (Aug 12 2026)' with ecm_eclipse = -18.22 * 0.95 * 1.672 ≈ -29 nT. inject_ai_layer.py: PRED-001 Ebro station with delta_Z = baseline(-10.9) * coverage(0.95) * latitude_factor(0.81) = -8.4 nT. WIN-025 listed as confirmed metadata entry ('observed: -10 nT') with no validation code.
code_search.notes: Two inconsistent formulations exist: monitor.py uses -18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ -29 nT; inject_ai_layer.py uses -10.9 × 0.95 × 0.81 = -8.4 nT. The -18.22 baseline in monitor.py equals -10.9 × 1.672 (BOU measurement × KAPPA), suggesting monitor.py applies KAPPA twice — once embedded in the baseline, once as the explicit multiplier. No derivation of why KAPPA should appear squared. No dome source computes coverage fractions from dome geometry — they are taken directly from globe eclipse predictions. The predictions page lists per-station FSF values claimed to derive from 'V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' but no computation code for these values was found in monitor.py, inject_ai_layer.py, or predictions.js.

suggested_text: The dome's eclipse prediction formula (ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF) reveals structural globe dependence in all three factors. The coverage fractions (ranging from 0.40 to 0.95 across 9 European stations) are eclipse obscuration percentages computed from Besselian elements — spherical Earth geometry that cannot be derived from a flat disc with local luminaries. The baseline (-10.9 nT or -22.24 nT) is a globe-measured INTERMAGNET observation. The FSF values, claimed to derive from dome geometry H(r)/r, require station radial positions computed from WGS84 coordinates via d_geo — itself a function of a = π × R<sub>Earth</sub>. The dome's own OPEN-001 concedes it has not derived dome-native coordinates without WGS84, meaning every input to the eclipse formula ultimately depends on globe infrastructure.
